summary: Powerhouse first set & soaring second‑set jams
blurb: The Hartford show delivers a tight, rocking first set highlighted by Althea, Big Railroad Blues and Let It Grow. The second set erupts into epic Space, Samson, Playing In The Band > Eyes and a relentless rhythm‑devils earthquake. Overall the band is locked in and the performance ranks among the ’82 high points.
review: April 18, 1982 at the Hartford Civic Center captures the Dead firing on all cylinders. Jerry Garcia roars through Let It Grow, Althea and Ship Of Fools with “amazing” solos and a voice that’s still strong after years of touring. Brent Mydland’s Hammond organ slashes over Big Railroad Blues and adds church‑organ texture to Cold Rain And Snow, while Phil Lesh delivers his famed “space quake” bass solo that shakes the room. The Rhythm Devils—Mickey Hart and Bill Kreutzmann—provide an “awesome”, mind‑bending drum landscape that fuels the second‑set marathon: Space, Samson & Delilah, a seamless Playing In The Band > Eyes Of The World, and a final burst of Black Peter into Sugar Magnolia. The soundboard capture (Miller) is crystal‑clear, preserving every nuance of this “powerhouse” set, making it the definitive listening source for anyone wanting to experience one of ’82’s most inspired nights.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Across six recordings, five are clearly positive and praise the same songs (Althea, Let It Grow, Space/Earthquake, Big Railroad Blues) and band members. Only one recording is mixed and offers no criticism of performance, resulting in a strong positive consensus that merits a high sentiment boost.

best_recording.reason: Crystal‑clear soundboard mix that captures every instrument and the full energy of the set, consistently described as “amazing board” and the definitive source for this show.

key_highlights: Tight, rocking first set with Althea, Big Railroad Blues and Let It Grow
Second‑set marathon: Space, Samson & Delilah, Playing In The Band > Eyes Of The World
Brent’s slashing Hammond organ on Let It Grow/Big Railroad Blues and church‑organ texture on Cold Rain And Snow
Rhythm Devils’ earthquake space segment driving the jam to a wild climax

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia delivers “amazing” solos on Let It Grow, Althea and Ship Of Fools; his voice remains strong and his lead work is described as sizzling and hot‑picking throughout the night.
band_performance.Phil: Lesh provides a powerful bass quake in Space and a thunderous Earthquake Space segment; reviewers note his playing is energetic though sometimes buried low in audience mixes.
band_performance.Bob: Weir’s vocal delivery shines on Althea and his slide work on Samson & Delilah earns praise, though a few listeners comment he sits more in the background on some recordings.
band_performance.Brent: Mydland’s Hammond organ slashes on Big Railroad Blues and Let It Grow; his church‑organ texture on Cold Rain And Snow adds depth, and overall his keyboard work is called “great” and “slashing”.
band_performance.Mickey: Hart helps craft the “awesome”, mind‑bending drum landscape that fuels the second‑set jams, especially during the Space/Earthquake sections.
band_performance.Billy: Kreutzmann’s drumming provides tight, nuanced support; his snare depth is highlighted in matrix mixes and contributes to the rhythmic drive of the entire show.
